# Transcrição: M2-launchthemes

- **Fonte:** M2-launchthemes
- **Duração:** 28:10
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Okay, so now I want to drill down into some of the common story themes that we use in our product launches. These things have been proven over the years, they work really, really well. They keep people engaged, they pull people into your market, and they really draw them into your launch and your world. One great big thing about these themes is they really help humanize our marketing. When you use these themes, people will really start to identify with you. In fact, they'll even want to beat you. One of the things that these stories do is they really put us on the same plane as the people watching our launch intellectually, emotionally, and socially. The deal is a lot of times if you're out there, I'm just having a business set you apart. It turns you into a guru or a star or some type of unattainable person. You don't really want that. You want people to be able to connect with you. You want that expert positioning, that guru positioning. You want to be seen as special, but you also want them to be able to connect with you. These stories really allow you to do that. You'll see as we go through these. Think about the movie Star Wars, for example. That movie is a classic hero's journey. Joseph Campbell's hero's journey. In fact, George Lucas, who created the movies, he very much based Star Wars on that hero's journey. In any case, Luke Skywalker, he ends up being this incredible hero in that movie, but he's the everyman. He starts off as just a boy and then he grows into this hero so you can identify with him. One thing that's important to remember as you go through these themes is you can mix and match these themes. You can use different themes in different parts of your launch. You can use more than one theme. You can have parts of your launch that don't have a theme at all, but I'll just say this. The more you put these themes to work in both in your launch and in your entire business, the better. They are extremely powerful. The more you base your story or your launch off of them, the better you're going to do. One final thing. I am a huge fan of being truthful in my marketing. It just makes everything a lot easier and more congruent. Authenticity really does win. People have that BS detector and it's a finely tuned mechanism. They can see right through in authenticity just like you wouldn't believe. So I'm a big fan of using these stories truthfully. The good news is that these themes exist everywhere in our lives and you're not going to have to look very hard to find a way to put these themes to work in your business. It's often just a matter of looking at our life in the context of these themes and then finding the ones that apply or don't apply at any given time.

## [03:00 - 06:00]

So let's get started. I'm going to start with my absolute favorite. This is my go-to one that I've probably used more than any others. And Reluctant Hero. The Reluctant Hero. I've used this one in my biggest launches, just some of my most successful launches and it works really, really well. I think one of the reasons I love this one is it's so aligned with my personality and my life. This Reluctant Hero story theme that was the basis of my first two really big six figure launches back when I was publishing about the stock market. One of them was when I published a home study course for stock market traders. And the deal is I had been publishing a stock market advisory letter for years. So that was my business was publishing a newsletter. The thing is, is our clients kept asking for us to basically we were putting out this letter saying, okay, this is going to happen in the market. And our stuff was really, really accurate. We published an amazing newsletter, but people wanted to know how to do it. So what I did was I started writing a book and that book literally took me years to write. Like I don't even, I probably, it was at least three years. It was just ridiculous how long it took me to finish that thing. I would never do it that way. Again, I learned a lot in the process, but it just took me forever to complete the writing. The research was hard. I really didn't want to do it. I just wanted to be publishing a newsletter. And, you know, because at the end of the day, I'm just, I'm a lifestyle focused person. You know, I love having a business. I love what the business does for me and my lifestyle, but I am very much about lifestyle. So when it was finally done or almost done, that was when I told, you know, I, it was time to do the launch. The reluctant hero story was just a natural. So what I, what I essentially said was, so I just got this email the other day and it was, it was from one of our long time readers and he's been following our, our market letter for literally a couple of years now. ## [09:02 - 12:03]

for a product before you actually create it. I used the reluctant hero again when I rolled out my first version of product launch formula, that version 1.0 back in 2005. And I wasn't ready to start talking to people. I wasn't ready to start consulting or coaching people. And that was the truth of it. But it also set up a very, very nice reluctant hero kind of story. Because a year later, when I ended up creating Product Launch Formula, I started teaching Product Launch Formula. It was just natural that I tell this reluctant hero story. It was like, I helped out some of the big gurus, the words snuck out. People started writing in and asking me, my phone was ringing off the hook. And I finally, the only way I could stop that phone from ringing off the hook was if I actually put this course together and started teaching it. So there's that reluctant hero. You know, I just, I didn't want to do this, but I was dragged kicking and screaming. And when you do this, the hero part isn't like you're some great big hero. Like that you're beating your chest that you're the big hero. The hero part is that you have the answer. You have the solution. You have something that could transform your viewers life. It's like you're the new sheriff in town and you're coming in to get rid of all the bad guys. What it is is you're the new sheriff and you've got this great solution that's going to help their lives. You don't really want to have to go through all the effort of putting a solution out and supporting it. But people really want this from you. They really need this help. They really need that transformation in their life. They need that change. They need that solution. And you putting in the effort to create that solution is the part that makes you the hero. So you're reluctant because you didn't want to have to do the work.

## [12:03 - 15:06]

You didn't want to put yourself in the limelight, but you're doing it just almost as a service to people. That's the essence of the reluctant hero story. It's very, very powerful. It's basically my go-to story. And the reason is it really fits my life. The reality is that I really am constantly torn between building my business and my impact further and just kicking back and spending more time with my family and more time in the mountains near my home. So reluctant hero, big fan of that one, it's worked really, really well for me. I've seen it work for a lot of other people when they put product launch formula to use in their business. Now the next theme is loss and redemption. And the reason I've got these stars up here, these are my favorites. So this is my extra double-starred favorite. So loss and redemption, this is a really powerful story. It's where maybe you had something great and then for some reason you lost it. And it's even better if someone took it away from you and then you turned it around and came back with it even bigger, better, and more powerful. There are a lot of Hollywood movies with this theme. A lot of times when you see this in business, especially maybe someone's teaching business, it's like they had a business and then they lost a business, they went bankrupt, something went wrong in the business, and then they came back and started again. Or maybe you were an athlete and you got injured and then you came back after your injury. Or maybe you were a guitar player and then you hurt your hand and you had to recover and in that recovery you learned things about teaching guitar and you were able to teach yourself and then you were able to teach other people. Now with loss and redemption, it turns you into a very sympathetic character. And people are going to root for you because you had something and you lost it. And especially if you lost it in some unjust or unfair fashion, they're going to root for you to get that back. Loss and redemption is very, very powerful. It's often a little harder to tie into a product launch than that reluctant hero. But often it's a smaller part of the launch. In other words, it's just a part of the bigger story that you'll work in that loss and redemption. But if you have a chance to use this in your launch, then it is an absolutely great theme. Now closely related to loss and redemption is the underdog story. People love to root for an underground. Nope, we're going to redo that and call that an underground. Although the whole idea of something being underground is pretty attractive too. It's the underdog.

## [15:06 - 18:11]

That common enemy is an amazing thing. People go to war and they kill each other over a common enemy. This actually ties into revenge. That's something we're going to get to down further. ## [18:11 - 21:12]

We had the mad scientist, you know, just coming up with something, discovering it. With the curation, this is where you went off and you studied all the great teachers and thought leaders and gurus. Maybe you've read all the books, went through all the trainings in your market or your niche. A perfect example of this is Napoleon Hill with his book, Thinking Roe Rich and everything that followed after that. In this case, he interviewed the 50 most successful men in the world. There's a whole story behind how he got to do that, but he interviewed all these really successful people. So that, and you'll see these two themes play out over and over, both the discovery and curation. They're both versions of an origin story. Usually our entire launch won't be based on either the discovery or the curation, but often that starts off your pre-launch. That's the beginning. That's the part of your story. Okay. We have, and I'm sure you've seen how you can mix and match these. I already just showed you how my origin story and reluctant hero can be, you know, basically tied together. You're using the discovery story within the reluctant hero story. Okay. So next is revenge. And this really isn't something that I use very much. I don't really like it because I don't think revenge, if you're seeking revenge, you're seeking revenge. It does not make you terribly attractive. The bottom line in our types of business, really any kind of business, is that we want to be naturally attractive. We want to attract people to us. We want to attract prospects to us, and we want to attract clients to us. And just revenge is just not a very, I mean, if you look at someone there going after revenge, it's not a very attractive way to be. And it's easy to be very unattractive when you're using revenge as a plot line. Now revenge is an extremely powerful emotion, and so it does make for a powerful story, but you have to be careful. It's usually used in marketing where there is that common enemy, and the common enemy could be an association or organization or government agency or big nasty competitors really trying to shut you out or, you know, keep you down, keep you out of the market. And what you want to do if you're going to use one of these revenge plot lines is you need to make sure that your revenge basically aligns with your prospects and their self-interest. So if your prospect wants something really badly, you have to make sure that the revenge that you're going after also gets them that thing that they want. And at the end of your revenge story, you really want to grow, you want it to grow beyond revenge. You want to, you know, to see a bigger picture, and you want to move towards service, you know, away from just hurting someone or getting back at someone or something towards, you know, maybe that's your initial impetus, but then as you go forward, then now you've grown

## [21:12 - 24:16]

and you really just want to serve people. Otherwise, the revenge is a real problem. And we never get sick of that story and it just really fascinates you. If you're going to use ascension, it tends to be a very personal story and it's going to be about you. Some people are really, they're less comfortable sharing their personal story. And if that's you, then you're going to have to watch out with the ascension story. I mean, if you use the ascension story, you really are sharing a story about yourself. My story is very much an ascension story. Now, I didn't use the ascension story when I was publishing about the stock market because I really, I didn't have an ascension story. Now, if you're going to use the ascension story, it's the best if there's some type of motivation, something that drives you to want to make that change. ## [24:16 - 27:09]

and even change the world through helping entrepreneurs. So there you have it. These are the seven main themes we've used over and over again in product launch formula.
